1: Cows of Bashan — parot ha-Bashan: the address to wealthy Samaritan women as cattle is sharply satirical. Bashan was celebrated for its fat, fertile cattle (Deut 32:14; Ps 22:12; Ezek 39:18). The image is of well-fed, complacent animals making demands: ha-omrot la'adonehem havi'u ve-nishteh (saying to their husbands, 'Bring us something to drink!') — the drinking-demand is the surface expression of an entire lifestyle built on the exploitation of the poor and needy (ha'osheqot dallim, harortsetsot evyonim).
2: The Lord GOD has sworn by his holiness — nishba' Adonai YHWH bequodsho: the divine oath formula with bequodsho (by his holiness) — the most absolute form of divine oath; YHWH swears by his own nature, which cannot be compromised. Ki hinneh yamim ba'im 'aleykhem (days are coming upon you) — the prophetic future; ve-nissa' etkhem be-tsinnot (they will drag you away with hooks/thorns) — tsinnah (thorn, hook; possibly fishing hook) — the image of fishhook deportation (cf. Ezek 29:4; 38:4 for the fishhook imagery).
3: Through the breaches in the walls — u-ferutsot tetse'na — they will go out through the breaches (perets) in the city walls made by the enemy assault, ishah neggdah (each woman straight before her), marched out in single file in the captive procession. Ve-hishlakhten ha-Harmonah — 'flung toward Harmon' — Harmonah is uncertain; possibly a proper place name for an exile destination or a textual crux. The image of exile is certain; the specific destination is not.
4: Come to Bethel and rebel — bo'u Vet-El u-fish'u ha-Gilgal harbu lifshoa': the biting sarcasm of this liturgical parody. Amos uses the form of a priestly call to worship (cf. Ps 95:1-2; 100:4) but replaces the sacred invitation with the accusation: pasha' (rebel, transgress) replaces worship. Bethel was the royal shrine of the north; Gilgal was an ancient cultic site near Jericho associated with covenant renewal (Josh 4-5; 1 Sam 11:14-15). Havi'u laboqer zivcheykhem (bring your sacrifices every morning) — the frequency of sacrifice is turned from piety to evidence of rebellion.
5: Offer your leavened thanksgiving sacrifices — ve-qatter mechamets todah: leavened offerings were prohibited from the altar (Lev 2:11) — either Amos is describing an actual illegal practice or using the leaven-prohibition symbolically for the corrupted thanksgiving offering. Ve-qir'u nedavot hash'mi'u (proclaim your freewill offerings, announce them publicly) — nedavah (freewill offering) is by definition supposed to be private and voluntary; publicizing it transforms it into performance of piety for social prestige. Ki ken ahavtem bene Yisra'el (for this is what you love to do) — the indictment: the motive is love of religious display, not love of God.
6: I gave you empty mouths — ve-gam ani natati lakhem niqyon shinayim: niqyon shinayim (cleanness/emptiness of teeth) — a vivid idiomatic expression for famine; nothing to eat = teeth that never get dirty. This begins the five-fold refrain: ve-lo shavtem elai ne'um-YHWH (yet you did not return to me, declares the LORD). Each judgment in the series (famine, drought, blight, plague, overthrow) was designed as a covenant-discipline calling for teshuvah (return/repentance), but none produced the response.
7: I also withheld the rain — ve-gam anochi manati mikkem et-hageshem: the covenant-curse of drought (Deut 28:23-24; Lev 26:19). The drought is spatially selective: ha'ir achat umtartem ve-ha'ir achat lo amtir (I sent rain on one city but not another) — demonstrating that the meteorological pattern was not natural but divinely governed. Chalqa achat yimmatertter ve-chalqa asher lo tammtir 'aleiha tivasesh (one piece of ground was rained on while another dried up) — even within the same district: YHWH controls the rain to a parcel-by-parcel level.
8: Two or three cities staggered — ve-na'u shtayim shalosh 'arim el-'ir achat: nu' (stagger/wander) captures physical weakness from thirst; they do not march to a city but stagger. Ve-lo yisbe'u (and could not get enough) — the water they found was insufficient. The famine and drought discipline aimed at shuv (return); it was not punitive destruction but corrective summons. Yet the refrain: they did not return.
9: I struck your crops with blight and mildew — hikiti etkhem ba-shiddafon uve-yerakon: shiddafon (blight, east wind damage; probably the hot sirocco that scorches grain) and yerakon (mildew, yellowing disease). Harbut gannotechem uve-kharmechem (your many gardens and vineyards) — the accumulation of crops signals prosperous agriculture, all of which the locusts (hazegav) consumed. The refrain: they did not return.
10: I sent among you a plague like those of Egypt — shilachti va-khem dever be-derekh Mitsrayim: the exodus-plague is the model for the covenant-curse judgment. Haragti va-cherev bacheireikhem (I killed your young men with the sword) — military defeat framed as divine killing. Bashvi suseikhem (took your horses captive) — the military establishment (horses, chariots) is captured. Va-a'aleh beosh machanekhem (I made the stench of your camps rise) — the stench of unburied dead, the ultimate defilement and disgrace. Refrain: they did not return.
11: I overthrew some of you as God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah — haphakhti vakhem ka'asher hafakh Elohim et-Sedom ve-et-Amorah: the Sodom-Gomorrah overthrow (hafakh = overturn, demolish) is the paradigm of total annihilation. Va-tehi khe'ud mutssal mi-sreifah (you were like a stick snatched from the fire) — the ud (burning stick, firebrand) rescued from fire — nearly destroyed; the metaphor recurs at Zech 3:2 for Joshua the high priest's near-destruction. Refrain: they did not return.
12: Prepare yourself to meet your God, Israel — lachen koh e'eseh lekha Yisra'el (therefore, because I will do this to you): the five-fold discipline has failed. The confrontation is now direct. Hikkon liqra'at Elohekha Yisra'el: hikkon (prepare yourself, be ready) — the Niphal imperative of kun (to be established, firm, ready); the same root is used for preparation before theophany (Exod 19:11, 15). Qara'at (to meet, toward meeting) — the encounter is forced, not invited. Israel will meet YHWH not as worshiper but as defendant.
13: For this is he who forms the mountains and creates the wind — the first of three doxological hymn-fragments in Amos (4:13; 5:8-9; 9:5-6). Yotser harim u-vore ruach (who forms the mountains and creates the wind) — the same two verbs of creation as in Gen 1-2: yatsar (form/fashion, as a potter) and bara' (create, the divine creative act used exclusively of God's activity). U-maggid le-adam mah-seho (who reveals to humanity what he is thinking) — seho = his thought/concern; the creator God who made the mountains also communicates with his creatures through prophets. YHWH Elohei Tzeva'ot shemo (the LORD God of armies is his name) — the doxology closes with the divine war-title; the creator is also the commander of all forces.
